lines.text: Against Me!. 'Sink, Florida, Sink.' 2007. New Wave. Laura Jane Grace wrote this one, and man, does it *land* at 04:00 in the morning.
Grace moved to Gainesville in '97, and that whole Gainesville scene — it infected her. This song is about watching your home state rot from the inside. I get it. I get it more than I should.

James Bowman's been playing guitar with her since 2001. Solid, reliable. Not flashy. Kinda like staying home on Purge night instead of running. You don't get credit for it, but you know you did the right thing.
Listen to the bridge. Just — listen. That's what despair sounds like when you're too tired to scream anymore.
The Bouncing Souls. 'Ole.' 1999. Hopeless Romantic. This one's for everyone still awake. For the people holding on.

New Brunswick, New Jersey. Four kids from Basking Ridge. Greg Attonito, Pete Steinkopf, Bryan Kienlen — they knew each other in high school. Built a label themselves, Chunksaah Records. Did it their way.
They call their fanbase 'True Believers.' Not ironic. Not a bit. They actually mean it. There's something — there's something sacred about a band that *believes* in the people listening.
One of the guys wrote 'Gone' when he was homeless. Heard a song in a parked car and it gave him hope. So he wrote this one to do the same for somebody else. That's the whole story right there.
'Ole' is a singalong. It's a *survival* song. If you're hearing this and you're home and you're scared — sing it. Just sing it.

tracks.title: A Better Place, A Better Time

lines.text: Hot Water Music. 'A Better Place, A Better Time.' 1997. Fuel for the Hate Game. Gainesville, Florida. Same city that made Laura Jane Grace. Something in the water down there.

Chuck Ragan and Chris Wollard — dual vocals. They don't harmonize. They *fight* with each other through the song. You can hear the tension. You can hear two people who care too much about the same thing.

The band almost broke up in '98. Touring exhausted them. They had a farewell show booked in Gainesville — August '98 — and during rehearsals for what was supposed to be goodbye, they wrote four new songs. They decided to keep going.
That's the moment. That's the moment everything changes. When you're supposed to end and you realize you can't. You *have* to keep going.

tracks.title: Sunday Morning
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Sunday Morning.' Cover song. 1997. These guys are a supergroup of burnouts playing other people's songs because their main bands won't let them have fun.
C.J. Ramone, Joey Cape, Fat Mike, Spike Slawson — they just decided one day: we're gonna take songs and punk the *shit* out of them. No concept album. No message. Just chaos in matching costumes.
Have a Ball was their first one. 1997. They covered '60s and '70s pop hits. Nobody was doing that. Nobody *should've* been doing that. And it worked because they didn't care.
This cover is tender. Actual genuine tenderness. Which is — which is worse, somehow, than if they'd shredded it. Tenderness hurts more at 04:00 in the morning.

The Lawrence Arms. 'Coming Home.' 2002. Apathy and Exhaustion. Brendan Kelly and Chris McCaughan. Chicago guys. Named the band after the apartment complex they got *evicted* from in the middle of the night.
They started by releasing a full-length album before they'd played a single show. A Guided Tour of Chicago, 1999. Just — boom. No EPs. No seven-inches. Just a record.
Kelly wrote a lot of these songs about drinking. About how alcohol flavors your whole life — good experiences and terrible ones all soaked in beer. He doesn't apologize for it. That's punk as hell.
'Coming Home' is about exhaustion and knowing you've done something that matters, even if nobody cares. Even if you never get credit. That's the song. That's all of it.
NOFX. 'The Decline.' 1999. Eighteen minutes. One song. This is the song they released as a standalone. As a *statement*.
Fat Mike formed this band in 1983 with Erik Sandin and Eric Melvin. Fairfax High School in Los Angeles. Named it after a Boston band called Negative FX. Just took the name and made it theirs.
NOFX never signed to a major label. Never. They saw the machine and they *didn't care* about it. That 'Leave It Alone' video in '94 — they made it and refused to send it to MTV. Said no thanks.

'The Decline' is fiery. Cynical. It's eighteen minutes of rage at the system, at the culture, at everyone complicit. And you listen to it at 04:00 and you realize the rage hasn't changed. It's the same rage. It's eternal.
This is the song people use to remember why they cared in the first place. Why punk mattered. Why staying home and saying no is a form of resistance.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 165 confirmed kills. {{ELAPSED}} elapsed. Pace trending 2 below 2168 — quieter night so far. East Side holding steady.
Text from Theresa, South Side: 'Tell Nick the Bouncing Souls saved my life six years ago. Still listening.' — Hear that, Nick?
Fog lifting off Mendota. Visibility improving to 6+ miles. Temperature holding at 22°F. No new shoreline incidents reported past 03:00.
